coredata.dc:title: Thermal runaway in large format semi-solid-state cells: Analysis of abuse test results and machine-learning-based core temperature prediction

coredata.dc:creator: Jimoh, Basit Asipita
Schieber, Christina
Odabaşı, Çağla
Kotak, Yash
Schweiger, Hans-Georg
coredata.dc:description: This research paper investigates the behaviour and thermal runaway (TR) conditions of large format semi-solid-state battery (SSSB) cells under several abuse tests like forced discharge test, overcharge test, external overheating test, external short-circuit test, nail penetration test, and crush test using the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standards and conditions beyond the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard. Based on the analysis of the abuse test results and the rating from the hazard severity level, it was revealed that SSSB cells exhibit improved thermal stability when compared to traditional lithium-ion battery cells due to the Lithium Phosphorous Sulphide coating of the Nickel-rich cathode which delays the release of oxygen. Furthermore, machine learning (ML) models (Random Forest, Extreme Gradient Boost, and Multiple Linear Regression) were trained to predict the core temperature for operating temperature range not more than 50 °C. For clarity, the predicted core temperature is the predicted center surface temperature. This core temperature is practically relevant because the tab regions are more accessible for sensing in battery modules/systems than placing additional sensors at the center region of every pouch cell. The random forest model best predicted the core center surface temperature of the SSSB cell with a root mean square error of 2.9 °C when compared to the experimental center surface temperature (T5), demonstrating the robustness of the model within the tested experimental domain. The results indicate that tabs' temperature measurements can serve as effective predictive indicators for the thermal state of the central surface region, offering a potential pathway for non-invasive thermal monitoring t and safety enhancement in SSSB systems.

head.ce:title: Thermal runaway in large format semi-solid-state cells: Analysis of abuse test results and machine-learning-based core temperature prediction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research paper investigates the behaviour and thermal runaway (TR) conditions of large format semi-solid-state battery (SSSB) cells under several abuse tests like forced discharge test, overcharge test, external overheating test, external short-circuit test, nail penetration test, and crush test using the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standards and conditions beyond the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard. Based on the analysis of the abuse test results and the rating from the hazard severity level, it was revealed that SSSB cells exhibit improved thermal stability when compared to traditional lithium-ion battery cells due to the Lithium Phosphorous Sulphide coating of the Nickel-rich cathode which delays the release of oxygen. Furthermore, machine learning (ML) models (Random Forest, Extreme Gradient Boost, and Multiple Linear Regression) were trained to predict the core temperature for operating temperature range not more than 50 °C. For clarity, the predicted core temperature is the predicted center surface temperature. This core temperature is practically relevant because the tab regions are more accessible for sensing in battery modules/systems than placing additional sensors at the center region of every pouch cell. The random forest model best predicted the core center surface temperature of the SSSB cell with a root mean square error of 2.9 °C when compared to the experimental center surface temperature (T5), demonstrating the robustness of the model within the tested experimental domain. The results indicate that tabs' temperature measurements can serve as effective predictive indicators for the thermal state of the central surface region, offering a potential pathway for non-invasive thermal monitoring t and safety enhancement in SSSB systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The emergence of hybrid solid-liquid batteries (HSLBs) plans to solve the shortcomings of traditional lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs); to improve the safety and energy density of LIBS and mitigate the chemo-mechanical interfacial problems in ASSBs [1–3]. To circumvent lithium dendrite formation safety issues in LIBs, solid-state electrolytes (SSE) were first proposed [4]. ASSBs showed that in theory, polymer SSE with enough shear modulus could block and prevent internal short-circuits (ISC) [5]. However, due to low ionic conductivity leading to slow charge/discharge rates, difficult and expensive manufacturing, and incompatibility with the anode or electrolyte the application of ASSBs is still limiting [6]. Additionally, the idea of SSEs not experiencing lithium dendrite formation was disproved for ceramic SSE, as grain boundaries offer the growth of pathways and lithium dendrites can also grow through SSE and eventually lead to ISC [7].

Based on structural designs and safety characteristics, HSLBs have been described using various terminologies by researchers, including “hybrid solid-liquid” [8], “semi-solid” [9,10], “quasi-solid” [11–13], “pseudo-solid” [14,15], and “nearly solid-state/almost solid-state” [16]. Some studies have collectively referred to both quasi-solid state and all-solid-state batteries as “solid-state” [8], which may lead to confusion. In addition, some researchers have used the term “all-solid-state” to describe “quasi-solid-state” [17], a practice that can be misleading or misinterpreted. For consistency's sake and to avoid ambiguity, HSLBs shall be addressed as semi-solid-state batteries (SSSBs) in this research.

By definition, SSSB cells employ the gelation of liquid solvents into solid polymer matrix to address the issues of flammable organic solvents and suppress cathode oxygen release in LIBs – and to improve poor ionic conductivity and unfavourable interfacial properties in ASSBs [11,18]. Safety designs based on multi-layer prevention of reactive oxygen, multi-functional separator and multi-level protection of lithium anode have been proposed for SSSBs to improve the intrinsic safety of this battery technology [8]. Nonetheless, achieving the intended safety enhancements will likely require a significant reduction in this liquid content. A study on SiOx-anode HSLB with composite polymer with oxide SSE showed poor performance when compared to traditional LIBs in an accelerated rate calorimetry (ARC) and external overheating tests, mainly due to the large volume changes of the SiOx anode causing uneven SEI formation during self-heating and uneven temperature distribution during external heating [19]. Another study compared the thermal runaway (TR) and gas generation characteristics of two commercially available large-formats batteries such as LIB and SSSB. The results showed that SSSB exhibited a slower temperature rise rate and lower concentration of hydrogen gas and hydrocarbons compared to the LIB, leading to the conclusion that the SSSB demonstrates superior safety [20].

Experimental results only from abuse tests in some cases could be insufficient as direct measurement of internal temperature is impractical due to integration constraints and cost; therefore, predictive models are essential. Hence, it becomes increasingly important to explore how machine learning (ML) techniques can be employed to infer thermally relevant temperatures from accessible measurements., particularly the core center surface temperature in a module design (where tabs' temperature measurement could easily be assessed than individual pouch center surface temperature), which is critical for evaluating thermal stability and mitigating thermal propagation. Recent advances demonstrate the power of data-driven methods such as artificial neural networks (ANNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs), long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, and hybrid models in modelling thermal dynamics of lithium-ion and next-generation batteries [21–23]. These models excel in handling time-series inputs and capturing nonlinear thermal behaviour caused by dynamic loads and aging effects. For example, RNNs when used for surface temperature prediction demonstrated significant improvements over conventional empirical models [21]. However, it was further highlighted that LSTM architectures outperform feedforward networks, especially under fluctuating environmental and usage conditions [22]. Recent review and experiment validate the role of ML in predicting core temperature using non-invasive surface measurements, it was emphasized that no single algorithm is universally superior, but deep learning models, especially ANNs and LSTMs, tend to offer a favourable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency when calibrated with well-structured datasets [23].

Moreover, hybrid models combining physical insights (e.g., thermal resistance-capacitance networks) with neural architectures have shown promise for scalable, real-time applications in battery thermal management systems (BTMS). Based on these findings, this research proposes a novel ML-based approach to estimate the core (center surface) temperature of SSSBs from tab temperature measurements. This method is intended as a non-invasive temperature inference approach for the thermally critical center surface region and does not claim direct reconstruction of the true internal electrochemical core temperature. The introduction of a thermal convolution method (TCM) integrated with physical modelling when validated via flight-based battery cycling, achieved <5% prediction error under dynamic conditions [24]. Similarly, the use of an adaptive neuro-fuzzy inference system (ANFIS) to predict temperature with a hybrid system identification model, further validated the ability to infer internal states from accessible external measurements [25]. Accurate core temperature estimation is not only vital for predicting critical thresholds like onset of self-heating, TR initiation, and propagation points, but also for identifying thermal gradients that can trigger localized degradation or safety failures. These temperature gradients, often invisible at the surface, can be revealed through intelligent inference models supporting real-time, predictive thermal management strategies. Such capabilities enable adaptive cooling, pre-emptive thermal throttling, and even early failure warnings [23,25]. Moreover, integration of ML-based core temperature prediction into battery management systems (BMS) enhances state of health (SoH) and state of safety (SoS) estimation capabilities [23,25]. These predictive insights support both operational safety and lifetime optimization, particularly in semi-solid-state technologies where thermal resilience is more sensitive due to unique material properties and narrower safety margins.

However, a significant gap remains in the comprehensive safety evaluation of large-format SSSBs, particularly through standardized electrical, mechanical and thermal abuse tests to understand critical TR parameters such as the onset temperature for self-heating, TR temperature, and maximum temperature of the flame during TR. ML plays a transformative role in this domain by enabling the non-intrusive prediction of center temperature, a parameter traditionally challenging to measure but essential for understanding internal heat accumulation. Furthermore, ML models can be expanded to predict temperature gradients across different points of the cell, providing early warning indicators for self-heating and propagation risks. Such predictive capabilities not only enhance real-time monitoring but also support adaptive thermal management strategies that can proactively adjust cooling mechanisms, preventing unforeseen thermal events before they escalate. By leveraging tab temperatures to infer core center surface thermal behaviours, ML-driven models offer a scalable solution for improving safety diagnostics, extending cell life, and optimizing thermal control in SSSB technologies.

The battery samples used in this research were commercially available large-format, semi-solid-state, pouch cells from an undisclosed manufacturer with a rated capacity of 35 Ah. The anode is a
SiO x
/graphite blended for its advantage in suppressing anode side reactions [26] and the cathode is a Ni-rich NMC with LPS coating. Table 1 briefly summarises the specification of cell that was used in the experiment.

The cell preconditioning was done at 20 °C room temperature in an ACS Compact DY200 Chamber (−40 °C /+ 180 °C, from ACS Angelamtoni, Martana, Italy) using a Neware BTS-4000 battery tester (5 V, 6 A, from Neware, Shenzhen, China). All cells' SoCs were adjusted to 100% using a CCCV- Charge current of 6 A (0.17C) with voltage cut-off of 4.2 V until charge current cut-off of 5 A (0.14C), except for forced discharge test were the SoC was rather adjusted to 0% using a CC-Discharge current of 6 A (0.17C) and voltage cut-off 2.75 V. Prior to the SoC adjustment, the cells were soaked in the room temperature for about 4 h.

The thermocouples used in all the abuse tests described in this research were the type K, NiCr-Ni (−100 °C to 1100 °C temperature range, sensor diameter 1.5 mm and element length 500 mm with Teflon and Cu shield cable type, Therma Thermofühler GmbH, Lindlar, Germany).
A, B, C, D, and R are the dimensions in mm, T1-T8 are the temperature points, while

∝ is the angle of inclination at 45°. Thermocouples were sticked to the cell surface using a grey butyl rubber putty sealant (−30 °C/+ 80 °C, self-adhesive, solvent-free, safe and durable, Teroson, Düsseldorf, Germany) at different temperature points (described in Table 2 and Table 3 ) as in Fig. 1 . The idea of having several points for temperature measurements is to understand how different points on the surface of the cell behave during abuse tests. In addition, several temperature points give allowance for redundancy in cases when thermocouples are disconnected from the cell during swelling or when one of the thermocouples get destroyed/faulty during measurements.

Table 4 highlights the different temperature configuration deployed in the abuse tests. Abuse tests like overcharge, forced discharge, and nail penetration use configuration_1 with temperature points T1, T2, T5, and T7. However, external overheating test, crush test and external short-circuit use configuration_2 and configuration_3, and configuration_4 respectively.

For crush test, the temperature points were chosen close to the circumference of the region of impact, such that the diameter of the region of impact is calculated from the center at ¾ of the cell width (87 mm). While for external overheating, additional temperature point T8 was used to understand the heat influx from the surface where the heat pad was applied to the other surface without the heat pad. For the sake of simplicity, the temperature points T1–T8 would be retained in the discussion of the results in this paper. The data from the thermocouples were logged using the data acquisition system (DAQ) (with I-87018Z I/O module with 16-bit resolution, 240 Vrms overvoltage protection, and up to 10 Hz sampling rate from ICP DAS, Houston, USA) for logging parameters like voltage, current, and temperature for all abuse tests except for forced discharge test, where the data were logged directly to the data acquisition system of the Neware BTS 4000 battery tester (5 V, 100 A, from Neware, Shenzhen, China).

The overcharge experiment was performed outdoors at an ambient temperature of 3 °C. The complete setup comprises EA-PS8080-40 T power supply (0/+80 V, 0/+ 120 A, EA Elektro-Automatik GmbH, Viersen, Germany) for charging, type K (NiCr-Ni) thermocouples with configuration_1 for temperature sensing, FLIR E96 thermal camera (−20 °C/+1500 °C range, IR lens: f = 10 mm, 42°1 range, f = 17 mm, 24°1 range, f = 19 mm, 14°1, 640 × 480 pixels, from Teledyne FLIR, Oregon, USA) for thermal videoing, GASMET PSP 4000 FTIR analyser (up to 50 gas multi-components matrix which includes reference spectra for the full ser of target compounds, fast response times <120 s, nitrogen zero-point calibration over 24 h, from GASMET technologies, Vantaa, Finland) for gas measurement, Canon XA30 (from Canon, Tokyo, Japan) for image videoing, and DAQ (with I-87017Rw for voltage and current logging and I-87018Z I/O modules for temperature logging, from ICP DAS, USA) at 1 Hz.

The preconditioned cells at 100% SOC were overcharged according to IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) [27,28] by applying a CC-Charge of 1C (35 A) until a cut-off voltage of 5.04 V, which is 120% of the cell's charging cut-off voltage. If no TR is observed with the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard condition, the cells would be immediately followed by another overcharging using a CC-Charge of 1C (35 A) until a cut-off voltage of 7 V. During the test, the cell voltage, current, and temperature were logged to the DAQ. In addition, the condition of the cell at the end of the test was noted and compared to the hazard severity level (HSL) rating described in [29].

The forced discharge test, also described as the overdischarge test was experimented outdoors at an ambient temperature of 5 °C. The setup consists of Neware BTS 4000 (5 V, 100 A) battery tester for overdischarging at 1 Hz, type K (NiCr-Ni) thermocouples with configuration_1 for temperature sensing, FLIR E96 thermal camera for thermal videoing, and Canon XA30 for image videoing.

The preconditioned cells at 0% SOC were overdischarged according to IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) [27,28] by applying a CC-Discharge of 1C (35 A) until a cut-off voltage of 0.91 V, which is 25% of the cell's nominal voltage. If no TR is observed with the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard condition, the cells would be followed immediately by another overdischarge using a CC-Discharge of 1C (35 A) until a cut-off voltage of 0 V. During the test, the cell voltage, current, and temperature were logged directly into the Neware BTS 4000 battery tester. The condition of the cell at the end of test was noted and compared to the HSL scale in [29].

External short-circuit (ESC) test simulates abuse condition of the SSSB cells if the positive and negative tabs accidentally make contact via a conductor. This test was conducted outdoor at an ambient temperature of 2.5 °C. Before the ESC test on site, the cells were kept in Bartscher AT90-ST oven (230 V/ 2.7 kW, Bartscher GmbH, Salzkotten, Germany) to maintain a temperature of 20 °C. The complete setup for this tests has GASMET PSP 4000 FTIR analyser for gas measurement, type K (NiCr-Ni) thermocouples with configuration_4 for temperature sensing, FLIR E96 thermal camera for thermal videoing, Canon XA30 for image videoing, LEM current sensors (ICP DAS, USA) for measuring the short-circuit current up to 1 kA, DAQ for logging parameters like voltage, current, and temperature at 10 Hz.

The test was conducted according to IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard [27,28] by connecting the positive and negative tabs of the preconditioned cell at 100% SoC with an external resistance (<5 mΩ) for 10 min. The total external resistance measured across the setup: cell (100% SoC), cables, copper plates, bolts, nuts, and the short-circuit relay system was 3.68 mΩ (meets the condition of <5 mΩ from the IEC 66260–273 (2022)) using Hioki 3554 Battery HiTester (3 mΩ to 3 Ω / 60 V, 4 ranges accuracy for resistance and 2 ranges accuracy for voltage, 1 kHz ± 30 Hz testing frequency source, from HIOKI E.E. Corporation, Nagano, Japan). The designed relay system can handle safely up to a disconnection current of 2 kA. The choice of cable was based on the theoretically computed short-circuit current for a cell with 35 Ah capacity and an externally applied resistance of 3.68 mΩ. The cells were bolted to the cables via copper plates to minimize resistance and prevent arcing on the tabs. The complete test setup is described in Fig. 2 . During the experiment, the cell voltage, short-circuit current, and temperature were logged to the DAQ. The condition and reaction of the cell at the end of test was noted and compared to the HSL scale from [29].

This test was performed to simulate a high-temperature environment that the cell may experience during abuse scenarios. The experiment was performed in an outdoor at ambient temperature of around 4.5 °C. For this test, the heat source was placed on the largest surface of the SSSB cell to understand the even flow of heat across the cell surface. From [30], it is understood that the position of the heat source is a major contributing factor to cell failure and onset of TR.

The over-temperature test setup shown in Fig. 3 consists of a silicone heat pad (230 V / 500 W, 145 mm × 195 mm, from Thermo Flächenheizungs GmbH, Rohrbach, Germany) as heat source, laptop with LabVIEW for controlling the temperature from the heat source, USB-4704 DAQ module (8 x Analog input channels, 12-bit and 14-bit resolution, sampling rate up to 10 kS/s, from Advantech Corporation, Taipei City, Taiwan), DMX dimmer (10 A/2200 W, from Highlite Internalnational B.V., Kerkrade, The Netherlands) as the controller, GASMET PSP 4000 FTIR analyser for gas measurement, type K (NiCr-Ni) thermocouples with configuration_3 for temperature sensing, FLIR E96 thermal camera for thermal videoing, Canon XA30 for image videoing, and DAQ for logging parameters such as voltage, current, and temperature at 1 Hz.

Based on the description from IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard [27,28], the SSSB cells preconditioned at 100% SoC was heated up at 5 °C/min with a controlled heat pad at one side of the cell surface (with the thermocouples in between the heat pad and the cell) until the temperature reached 130 °C. Then this 130 °C temperature was held for 30 min to observe the behaviour of the SSSB cells. However, if no TR is reached using the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard, the cell shall be continually heated until the TR condition is reached. During the test, the cell voltage and temperature were logged to the DAQ. The condition and reaction of the cell at the end of the test was noted and compared to the HSL scale described in [29].

The test was carried out in an outdoor setting where the ambient temperature was at 3.5°C. However, the SSSB cells were placed in a Bartscher AT90-ST oven onsite to keep the cell temperature close to 20 °C room temperature before the nail penetration test. For the nail penetration was conducted based on the state-of-the-arts. Instead of nail penetration speed of 2 cm/s and 8 cm/s used in [21,32] respectively, a slower speed of 0.05 cm/s was used to simulate the least harsh condition that a single cell could face.

The preconditioned SSSB cells at 100% SOC placed on an insulated testbed were penetrated at the center by the 6 mm nail head which travelled vertically downward at 0.05 cm/s. After the test, the condition of the SSSB cells were rated accordingly using the HSL scale [29].

The crush test was used to simulate the effect of external forces that may lead to deformation of the SSSB cells. This test was done outdoor at ambient temperature of 3.5 °C but prior to the test, all SSSB samples were kept in the Bartscher AT90-ST oven to ensure the temperature of the samples were kept close to 20 °C room temperature prior to the crush test.

The experimental setup comprises the following; The same inhouse designed press system used for nail penetration (The press is fully described in 2.3.5 by Table 5 and Fig. 4), but instead of a nail, a 130 mm diameter, 7 kg spherical steel impactor was used to exact the required force at a speed of 30 mm/min, type K (NiCr-Ni) thermocouples with configuration_2 for temperature sensing, Hioki 3554 Battery Hitester for resistance measurement, FLIR E96 thermal camera for thermal videoing, Canon XA30 for image videoing, and DAQ for logging parameters like voltage, current, and temperature at 10 Hz. The mass, voltage, temperature, and resistance of the SSSB cells were measured and recorded before, during, and after the test.

According to IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standards [27,28], the SSSB cells preconditioned at 100% SoC were kept on an insulated surface and a force via the impactor was applied perpendicular to the cell surface at a speed of 0.05 cm/s until a condition of 1000 times the weight of the cell (> 4200 N) or a 15% or more deformation (> 1.65 mm) in the cell was reached. The condition of the SSSB cells after the test was rated based on the HSL ratings described in [29].

The aim of the computational analysis is to help predict the core center surface temperature of the cells based on the different temperature points selected in the experimental abuse data set conducted in section 2.3. The computational methodology used in this study involved data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, and the development and evaluation of three predictive models: Multiple Linear Regression (MLR), Random Forest (RF), and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost). The primary objective of these models was to predict the core center surface temperature, an equivalent of the experimental surface temperature T5 of the cells based on temperatures measured positive table (T1) and near the negative table (T2) under abuse tests. Accordingly, the model should be interpreted as an inference tool for the cell's central surface region rather than as a direct estimator of the true internal electrochemical core temperature.

The experimental dataset from the abuse tests were utilized in model development and evaluation was initially loaded from an Excel file. To ensure data integrity and consistency for subsequent analysis, a thorough preprocessing phase was conducted. This involved grouping the experimental dataset by unique test identifiers to represent the test scenarios, and then further splitting each test group into individual datasets to facilitate independent and detailed analysis specific to each test scenario.

Descriptive statistics and exploratory data visualizations were performed. Time-series plots were generated to investigate trends and fluctuations in voltage and temperature for each test condition. Two primary visualizations were constructed to analyse voltage and temperature variations under different conditions.

For predicting core center surface temperature based on T1 and T2, the dataset was meticulously filtered. Only experimental dataset that showed TR such as overcharge test (electrical), external overheating test (thermal), and nail penetration test (mechanical) were retained. To mimic realistic operating conditions and exclude extreme outlier events, data points where temperature values exceeded 50 °C were removed. In addition, it should be noted that several abuse experiments were performed outdoors under relatively low ambient temperatures of approximately 2.5–5 °C, although in some cases the cells were preconditioned close to room temperature before the test. Therefore, the learned temperature relationships reflect the specific environmental conditions of the present experiments, and the resulting ML model should be interpreted as condition-specific rather than automatically generalizable to standard room-temperature operation without further validation.

A stratified sampling approach was implemented to divide the filtered dataset into training and test sets. This ensured that the test set constituted 20% of each test type, thereby preserving the original distribution of samples. The remaining 80% of the data was allocated to the training set, serving as the basis for model development.

The MLR model was employed to establish a linear relationship between core center surface temperature and the temperatures recorded at T1 and T2. This foundational model was implemented in R using an Ordinary Least Squares approach, with the formula expressed as core center surface temperature ~ (T1 + T2). This formulation aims to understand the correlative influence of the tab temperatures on the cell's internal temperature. The MLR model was chosen as an initial approach due to its simplicity and interpretability, providing a baseline for comparison with more complex models. It is acknowledged that MLR models assume linearity, independence of errors, homoscedasticity, and normality of residuals, which will be considered during the model evaluation phase. The performance of this model will be assessed using metrics such as Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) during cross-validation and on the independent test set.

The RF model was employed as an ensemble learning method for predicting core center surface temperature. This model was constructed using R and configured with 500 decision trees. To enhance the model's robustness and prevent overfitting, a minimum node size of 5 was specified. Additionally, random feature selection was employed at each split, contributing to the diversity of the individual trees within the forest. The Mean Decrease in Gini Impurity criterion was utilized for splitting nodes, a metric that quantifies each feature's contribution to the homogeneity of the resulting nodes.

To assess the relative contribution of the input features to the model's predictions, variable importance scores were extracted from the trained RF model. This analysis provided insights into the impact of both T1 and T2 temperature on core center surface temperature. The feature importance evaluation, derived from the Mean Decrease in Gini Impurity metric, highlighted which features played a critical role in predicting temperature variations. This information was crucial for aiding informed decisions regarding feature selection and model refinement. The computed importance scores were ranked and subsequently visualized using bar plots to clearly illustrate their relative significance.

The XGBoost model, recognized as a powerful ensemble learning method, was employed to predict core center surface temperature. This model was implemented in R and configured with 100 boosting rounds. Key hyperparameters included a learning rate of 0.1 and a maximum tree depth of 6. To enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting by reducing variance, a subsample ratio of 0.8 and a colsample_bytree value of 0.8 were applied. XGBoost iterative nature allows for the sequential correction of errors from preceding trees, which typically leads to superior predictive accuracy compared to simpler models.

A 10-fold cross-validation approach was implemented on the training set to rigorously evaluate the model's performance. Given the time-series nature of the dataset, folds were split sequentially. This approach ensured the maintenance of temporal dependencies, thereby assessing each model's performance on future data it had not encountered during training. The RMSE was computed for each model within every fold to quantify predictive accuracy.

After completing cross-validation, each model was trained on the full training set and subsequently evaluated using the designated, independent test set. RMSE values for the test set were computed, offering a comparative assessment of the models' generalization capabilities to truly unseen data. The overall modelling workflow is summarized in Fig. 5 , which illustrates the data preprocessing, sampling, model development, and evaluation steps.

To visually represent model performance and insights, scatter plots were generated at the end. These plots compared the actual experimental center surface temperature, T5 versus predicted core center surface temperature values for each model on the final test set. Additionally, feature importance plots were created to visually illustrate the relative significance of predictor variables specifically within the RF model. These comprehensive visualizations provided clear insights into overall model performance and the influence of the input features.

The behaviour of the cell to overcharge abuse is described in detail in five stages I-V in Fig. 6 while the dynamic evolution is described in Fig. 7 . During stage I, CC-charge of 1C was applied and the voltage started increasing linearly from 4.16 V until the first voltage inflection point (VIP) occurred at 5.21 V, and shortly a voltage peak at 5.36 V after 156 s of overcharging. The appearance of the VIP and presence of Lithium plating at the anode could be associated to dissolution of manganese and release of oxygen at the cathode, as reported in prior studies [33–37]. Due to the reactions, internal resistance and gas generation started [34]. In stage II, the peaked voltage from point A started dropping, signifying the anode potential reaching its peak, as indicated in [38]. This stage also marked the beginning of the rise in temperature due to the cell's internal resistance [35,37]. The temperature started rising rapidly due to the carbon anode layer being intercalated with excess lithium. Hence, leading to lithium deposition at the anode surface [37]. The deposited lithium would react with SEI at the anode chemically to engage side reactions and ohmic heat generation, as shown in [39], contributing to further increase of the temperature. The formation of voltage platform or plateau was observed, which may be from the persistent stabilization of internal micro-short-circuit current and charging current in mostly large format Nickel-rich LIBs [36,40,41]. In stage III, swelling became most prominent and this could be due to the lithium plating/lithium dendrite, as reported in [42] and/or gas produced by the incomplete oxidation of electrolyte, as documented in [43]. At point B, the cell deflated and started venting lightly, this feature is one of the safety attributes of pouch cells during gas formation since pouch cells do not have safety valves [37]. The steep rise in voltage marks the end of reversible lithium intercalation and beginning of irreversible side reactions due to full delithiation of the cathode [34,35]. In the ultimate phase, stage IV, the cell seems to experience another voltage plateau, which again was because of several side reactions consuming all the current as the cathode was already fully delithiated and the resistance had tremendously increased. The voltage reached a maximum of 7.00 V after 741 s and then suddenly plummeted to 1.06 V just 2 s later due to a severe ISC, hence the cell is described to have completely failed electrochemically. The temperature rose exponentially, and large venting started emanating from the ruptured spot. 10 s after the cell failure, there was a massive explosion at high pressure. This 10 s before explosion marks the last warning sign in the TR chain.

An interesting finding was that the temperature at which TR starts is influenced by the surrounding environment. When charging at low ambient temperatures, the charging process lasts longer, and both internal heat generation and external heat help warm the cell in the early stage, leading to an increase in its capacity, consistent with findings from [44]. The early warning signal before TR is always after the point of inflection - the voltage plateau, observed in [45] and sometimes occurrence of a voltage trough [41].

Summarily, the TR in this case was suspected to have been initiated by ISC from dendrites due to the overcharging at high c-rate of 1C and at low temperature and further aggravated by melting and shrinking of the separator. According to the HSL scale, the cell was rated 2 according to IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard but rated 7 for failure beyond the applied standard at voltage around 7 V.

At a CC-discharge of 35 A, the voltage first dropped rapidly to 0.91 V cutoff voltage without a considerable change in the cell's temperature. However, further force discharged showed that the cell's voltage rapidly dropped to 0.4 V and the cells started heating up. The reason for the sudden rise in temperature of the cells could be as a result of the onset dissolution of the organic component of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) as lithium is being overly depleted in the anode, as established in [46]. On the cell surface (cell no1), the temperature trend as in Fig. 8 follows that temperature at the center (T5) and below (T7) increased faster than those closer to the tabs (T1 and T2), with a similar trend reported in [46,47].

After 600 s, the voltage dropped to almost 0 V and the surface temperature was seen to peak. In most cases, the peak temperature was always seen either in the center temperature, T5 or below temperature, T7, see Fig. S10, Fig. S12, and Fig. S14 – Though in no case was any of the surface temperature of the SSSB cells recorded beyond 55 °C. Unlike in literature with LIBs having more than 60 °C surface temperature, as reported in [46–48]. These recorded surface temperature of the SSSB cells in this work is therefore not threatening enough to drive the exothermic reaction to the TR condition. From the Video recording, it was observed that there was slight swelling in the cell. In addition, the cells' resistance prior to test and 10 min after test showed an increase about 60–84%, further corroborating the dissolution of SEI. The cells were rated 2 according to HSL score.

The ESC test in Fig. 9 could be classified into three unique stages based on the evolution of voltage, short-circuit current, and temperature. In stage I, large short-circuit current of peak 982 A (equivalent to a discharge C-rate of 28C) was observed, accompanied by a sudden voltage drop to 1.65 V which could be due to pure ohmic and faraday charge transfer resistance, similar to the findings in [49,50]. There was a sharp voltage recovered due to Li+ ions accumulating on the surface of active particles and increasing concentration polarization, but as the temperature rose, the increased ion conductivity led to reduced polarization and a sharp rise in voltage; however, this temperature-induced increase was not enough to offset the voltage loss caused by internal resistance, resulting in a gradual decline in voltage over time [50]. Then the voltage tried to stabilize leading to a quasi-plateau voltage and the cell started swelling and venting. Swelling reached maximum after 46 s and electrolyte started leaking. The FTIR detected Ethyl Methyl Carbonate (EMC), Dimethyl Carbonate (DMC), and Diethyl Carbonate (DEC) components of the organic liquid electrolytes in a quantity of 571.22 ppm, 2.39 ppm, and 48.86 ppm respectively. Temperature also rose quickly - the tabs temperatures responded faster than the center temperature since joule heating was more concentrated at the tabs and the cell laminate had poorer conductivity compared to the tabs, similar findings were discussed in [51]. However, the peak temperature was observed on the surface of the cell of about 75 °C, which was not sufficient to drive the cell into TR. When test was repeated, a similar trend was observed in the voltages (Fig. S15 and Fig. S16) and a peak surface temperature of about 100 °C (Fig. S17), which also did not drive the cell into TR condition.

In stage II, between time 400–600 s as the short was still maintained, a stepwise flat-region resembling a voltage plateau corresponding to 0.9 V, 0.8 V, and 0.7 V were formed. These false plateau, unlike the true plateau formed from two-phase equilibrium in stage I, were from transport-limited bottleneck which happened when Li+ movement caused local electrolyte depletion and resistance buildup at the anode interface, which temporarily stabilized the voltage into a false plateau. This transport-limited regime could also contribute to Joule heating and hot spots, in agreement with [52]. After 10 min, the external short-circuit was removed and the behaviour of the cell potential could be seen to relax in stage III from 1.31 V and went on to reach around 3.5 V, indicating that the cell had not been fully discharged from the effect of ESC. Fig. S24 shows the ballooned and venting cell. The safety performance under extreme discharge current via external resistance was passed by the cell with an HSL rating of 4.

Since it is quite difficult to know the internal temperature of the cell components, especially the electrolyte during the external overheating test, it makes sense to analyse the temperature of the surface without the heat source, T8, in relation to the temperature of the surface where the heat source was placed, T5. Two critical temperature points were defined as
T tr
, and
T max
in existing literature [13,19], where
T tr
stands for triggering temperature and
T max
means the maximum temperature at TR. Fig. 10 describes the three stages in the external overheating test. In stage I, the heat source applied a ramp temperature of 5 °C/min until 130 °C. Part of the heat was lost to convection due to the ambient temperature being extremely low. However, substantial part of the heat was gained by the cell. The cell in this stage was stable and showed no physical reaction.

In stage II, the heat source at 130 °C was constant for 10 min. The cells' temperature T5 and T8 were constant, but the cell bulged a bit. The end of stage II attested to the cells passing the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022), since no fire or explosion was seen during the test. According to HSL in [29], the behaviour of the cell was rated 2.

Fig. 11 shows an enlarged version of stage III for one of the cells. The heat source was then raised from 130 °C to 300 °C (cells failed at 300 °C) at a rate of 18 °C/min. Venting started and the cell ruptured, releasing electrolyte vapor and other gases which led to a slight drop in the T8 despite an increase in T5. Such drop in T8 under increased T5 is termed as the first warning signal for safety measures. As the temperature of the heat source further increased, SEI decomposed and lost its protective layer, leading to the anode reacting with electrolyte to further contribute to the heat release. This finding is corroborated by [53]. Due to the enormous heat release, T8 experienced a change in temperature rate greater than 1 °C/s.

This sudden increase in the rate of T8 (> 1 °C/s) defined the second warning signal and also defined the triggering temperature for TR, as mentioned in [54]. Two seconds later, the open circuit voltage (OCV) spiked quickly from 4.129 V to 4.94 V and then dropped to 0.1 V due to the ISC of the anode and cathode from the shrinking and melting of the separator [30,55]. The ISC defined in [55], as a change in the OCV at a rate greater than 0.1 V/s corroborated the cell failure and marked the warning signal 3 (which is the last warning signal). There was only 20 s in between the second warning signal to point of explosion. During the explosion, the maximum TR temperature was recorded to be 912.72 °C and maximum temperature of the flame went up to 1141 °C as shown in Fig. S29. In conclusion, the cells are said to maintain thermal stability during stages I and II corresponding to the 5 °C heat ramp to 130 °C and waiting time respectively. The behaviour of the other during 30 min and 5 min waiting time are given in Fig. S26 and Fig. S31 respectively.

Based on the local conditions inside the cell, the ISC introduced in nail penetration could either be hard (severe) or soft (sluggish). This ISC could lead to the type of jet flame observed for large pouch cells. Due to the test setup adopted, all cells tested went into TR and most of the ISC observed were hard types.

In Fig. 12 , the OCV first transient to 4.99 V in 0.26 s (t2 - t1) when the nail was introduced. Then it began to drop quickly after the ISC started and showed some fluctuations. In just 4.37 s (t3), the transient OCV dropped to 0.50 V, pointing to a lower internal resistance, which allowed a higher short-circuit current and faster energy release. The steeper voltage decline matched well with the strong jet flame and the more rapid heating at the center, T5 of 79 °C. In this case, the short circuit appeared to have been more severe, producing stronger electro-thermal feedback that pushed the cell more abruptly toward failure. As a result, the pouch cell released the significant amount of heat via its thin laminate which was deliberately designed to rupture easily for safety reasons. The jet flame was lateral, and heat was not accrued in the cell for a long time, the FLIR measured a maximum hotspot value of 991 °C for the flame, see Fig. S35.

The situation is different in Fig. 13 , where the voltage remained almost flat at approximately 3.95 V for most of the ISC duration and then fell abruptly to 0 V after about 8.75 s. This relatively long plateau suggested that the effective short resistance in this test was higher, so the current was limited, and the voltage collapse was delayed. In other words, the short circuit effect was seen to be “sluggish or soft” compared to Fig. 12. However, the center temperature, T5 still climbed to about 163.75 °C. The penetration hole in this case was sufficient to jet out the flame without the thin laminate rupturing. The cell experienced considerable thermal stress. The plateau behaviour therefore reflected a more gradual internal degradation process, where local heating weakened the separator until it finally failed. Hence, the maximum hotspot for the jet flame was 1151 °C, see Fig. S38. Similar firecracker-like jet flames are predominant in cylindrical cells, as noted in [56–58], rather than in pouch cells, owing to the protective material structure and the smaller venting outlets characteristic of cylindrical cells.

Despite nominally identical conditions, is not surprising. Small changes in nail penetration depth, angle, or contact area can strongly affect the short resistance. Similarly, cell-to-cell variability in electrode alignment, electrolyte distribution, or even SEI condition could alter the way the ISC develops. Once heating begins, the feedback between temperature rise and internal resistance further amplifies these differences. Since all the cells reacted violently with fire and explosion during this test, the HSL rating is given as 7.

The evolution of force and deformation because of the impactor is shown in Fig. 14 while Fig. 15 shows the impact of voltage and temperature during the test. The force and deformation increased linearly. The IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard conditions were met a deformation of 1.6 mm (>15% of the cell's thickness) and a force of 4.23 kN (>1000 times cell weight) were reached; however, no change in the cell's voltage or temperature was observed.

The impact of force continued, and more deformation was caused in the cell. The cell's force reached 50.3 kN (approximately 10,000 times cell weight) and a deformation of 9.44 mm (approximately 85% of the cell's thickness). However, the voltage and temperature remained unchanged. The pressure caused by the impactor around the impact region was not sufficient to cause ISC in the pouch cell.

After the test, the internal resistance was measured to be 0.9 mΩ, also unchanged. The cell passed the crush test with an HSL rating of 2 during both the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard phase and far beyond the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard since there was no sign of fire and explosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Core center surface temperature prediction discussion
ce:section.ce:para: The model's inputs were the positive and negative tab temperatures, while the output was the predicted center temperature. The dataset was split into training (80%) and test (20%) subsets, with the training set used for 10-fold cross-validation and the test set reserved for evaluating performance on unseen data. Three ML techniques, MLR, RF, and XGBoost were employed to predict the center temperature from tab measurements.

According to the results presented in Table 6 , the RF model achieved the lowest RMSE on both cross-validation and test sets, demonstrating superior predictive performance. The actual versus predicted values is illustrated in Fig. 16 show that most data points align closely along the y = x line, indicating a generally high level of accuracy. However, the predictive accuracy is not perfectly uniform across the full temperature span. In the moderate range of approximately 15–33 °C, where batteries commonly operate, the thermal gradients between the tabs and the center surface are comparatively small; consequently, noise measurement, local heat spreading effects, and sample imbalance can represent a larger fraction of the observed variation. This likely makes prediction errors more visible in that interval than in more strongly heated regimes, where the relationship between input and output temperatures becomes more pronounced.

To further investigate the influence of input features on the prediction model, the increased in Node Purity (IncNodePurity) values were analysed from the RF model. The IncNodePurity metric measures the decrease in node impurity (e.g. variance) when a feature is used for splitting, provides insight into the relative importance of each input variable.

These results of the IncNodePurity presented in Fig. 17 indicate that both input features contribute significantly to the model's predictive capability. However, T1 has a notably higher IncNodePurity, suggesting it is the more influential variable in predicting the center temperature. This could be due to the asymmetric thermal behaviour of the cell, where heat dissipation patterns and internal resistance differences lead to a stronger correlation between the positive tab temperature and the predicted core center surface temperature. Hence, this analysis confirms that tab temperatures are reliable indicators for estimating core center surface temperature, with the positive tab playing a slightly more dominant role in prediction accuracy.

The successful prediction of core temperature from external tab measurements holds significant implications for battery safety and thermal management systems. In real-world battery modules, where adding sensors at the center region of every pouch cell may be impractical because of cost, packaging, wiring complexity, or integration constraints, this model provides a scalable and non-invasive alternative using more accessible tab measurements. In this sense, the model estimates the thermal state of the thermally critical central surface region, not the true internal electrochemical core temperature. By leveraging tab temperatures, BMS can infer whether abnormal heat buildup is developing at the cell center surface and can support proactive mitigation strategies such as dynamic cooling, load reduction, or emergency shutdown. At the same time, because the model was trained on abuse-test data collected under a relatively narrow and low ambient temperature range, its present applicability to common room-temperature scenarios should be regarded as limited until broader validation datasets are incorporated. The model should therefore be interpreted as a proof-of-concept and preventive early-warning tool within the tested domain.

As seen in the results from the abuse tests, ISC is believed to be the main reason for battery failure. Following the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard, all the cells passed the abuse test with the safest seen with the forced discharge and crush test with HSL 2, followed by ESC, overcharge and external overheating tests all with HSL rating of 4. However, beyond the IEC 62660–2/3 (2022) standard, only crush and forced discharge tests were passed by the cells. Overcharge proved to be the worst case for electrical abuse tests with HSL rating of 7. External overheating also failed when the temperature from the 500 W heat pad went above 300 °C. The nail penetration test failed with HSL 7 despite the low speed of 0.05 cm/s of the nail. The summary of the HSL for the abuse tests is shown in Fig. 18 .

The safety features associated with cathode coating of the SSSBs could be seen in the different abuse conditions despite the thermal instability associated with the Ni-rich cathode. The coating of the cathode with LPS is believed to suppress the cathode interfacial reactivity. Hence, the following:
•
In overcharge test: Delayed onset of TR and allowed a 10 s warning time between the point of ISC and when it exploded; this delay could be attributed to LPS coating reduced parasitic reactions, suppressed oxygen release, and improved interfacial stability i.e. lowered heat generation and slowed the escalation of internal shorts.
•
In forced discharge test: The SEI in this case is understood to be still stable and no internal side reaction is sufficient to drive the cell to TR.
•
In external short-circuit test: The influence of the external short-circuit could not drive the cell to TR despite the increase in the surface temperature of about 90–100 °C. It is believed that the cathode coating suppressed oxygen release and hence, no further chain reaction was observed to drive the cell to fire/explosion.
•
In external overheating test: Temperature, T7 rate change >1 °C/s defined the TR triggering temperature and the second warning signal. ISC was confirmed from the voltage drop 2 s after TR triggering. But not until 20 s after ISC that the cell exploded, and this delay to explosion could be attributed to the cathode coating which suppressed the release of oxygen.
•
In nail penetration test: This test introduced the fastest ISC into the cell material. Since the ISC happened very fast for nail penetration, it is quite difficult for it to be suppressed. Unlike the ISC from overcharge and external overheating, the ISC from the nail penetration test is nearly complicated as the steel nail itself acts as a thermal and electrical sink during the test [59]. In fact, for certain standards, nail penetration is not considered fully representative of real-world accident scenarios [60].
•
In crush test: A force up to 50 kN from a 7 kg spherical steel impactor of diameter 130 mm left about 85% indentation on the cell thickness without ISC or noticeable voltage and temperature change.

Overall, despite the LPS coating of the cathode able to suppress TR event by improving thermal stability, there is a need to reduce the contents of liquid electrolyte with organic solvent in SSSBs, or better still all-solid state should be given priority for the design of next generation battery. Moving toward fully solid-state architecture will eventually allow for the reduction or elimination of certain safety redundancies, such as heavy active liquid-cooling infrastructure and complex gas exhaust channels, due to the absence of volatile liquid solvents. For the development of large format SSSBs module and system, it is recommended to use of pressure plates encouraged as they help with performance and could improve safety by shutting down cell to cell thermal propagation. In addition, BMS should include adaptive charging to suppress dendrites and integration of pressure/gas/temperature sensing for predictive safety control. Finally, standards should evolve toward reproducible, worst-case testing (often at high SoC), deeper failure-mode characterization when TR is not reached, and explicit propagation/fire-resistance assessment at module and pack scales.

RF showed the best performance with RMSE values of 7.92 (cv) and 2.91 (test) when compared to 9.7/10.3 for MLR and 8.52/3.02 for XGBoost. This result further confirms that tab measurements, with positive tab being more dominant, are reliable indicators of heat accumulation at the thermally critical center surface region and can support real-time thermal monitoring below 50 °C. However, the model should not be overinterpreted as a universal predictor of true internal electrochemical core temperature or as a model already validated for all ambient conditions. Because the training data were derived from specific abuse experiments, many of which were conducted at low outdoor ambient temperatures, the current model is best interpreted as a condition-specific preventive diagnostic tool for early warning within the tested experimental domain.

Following onto these observations and conclusion by the authors, the future research work should focus on thermal propagation from cell to cell in modules and packs under different abuse conditions. For the ML-based core center surface temperature prediction, broader ambient temperature coverage and dedicated temperature-band error analysis to improve robustness under room temperature operating conditions.
